theory.title: Theoretical Guide to Scales
theory.subtitle: Discover how scales are used across different genres, which famous songs use them, and practical tips for improvisation.

bluesRock.title: Blues & Rock
bluesRock.desc: The heart of Blues is the Minor Pentatonic, enriched with the 'blue note' (b5). Add the Major Pentatonic on dominant chords for a sweet-bitter contrast. In Rock, the Natural Minor and Mixolydian scales add power to riffs.

jazzFusion.title: Jazz & Fusion
jazzFusion.desc: Jazz requires scales to accompany complex harmonic progressions. Dorian on minor chords (IIm7), Mixolydian on dominants (V7), Ionian on major (Imaj7). Fusion adds the Lydian Dominant and the Altered scale for chromatic tension.

metalHardRock.title: Metal & Hard Rock
metalHardRock.desc: Aggressiveness comes from semitones close to the tonic. The Phrygian and Harmonic Minor scales offer a dark and neoclassical sound. The Locrian is perfect for riffs on diminished chords.

popClassical.title: Pop & Classical Music
popClassical.desc: Pop and Classic Rock thrive on the Ionian (Major) and the Major Pentatonic for bright melodies. Aeolian (Natural Minor) is the go-to scale for emotional ballads. In classical music, Harmonic Minor and Melodic Minor add harmonic complexity.

filters.label: Filter by genre:
filters.noResults: No scales found for this genre.

mixMajorMinor.title: Mixing Major and Minor
mixMajorMinor.desc: One of the most iconic techniques of blues and rock is to alternate between the Minor Pentatonic and the Major Pentatonic over the same chord. This creates a 'sweet and bitter' contrast that is the hallmark of guitarists like B.B. King and Jimi Hendrix.
mixMajorMinor.proTipDesc: On an A7 chord, try playing A Minor Pentatonic for tension and then resolve on notes from A Major Pentatonic. The magic note is the Major 3rd (C#) played after the minor 3rd (C).

chordScale.title: Playing Over Changes (Chord-Scale)
chordScale.desc: Instead of using a single scale for the whole song, advanced guitarists change scale with each chord. On a IIm7-V7-Imaj7 in C: use D Dorian on Dm7, G Mixolydian on G7, and C Ionian on Cmaj7. The notes change subtly, following the harmony.

passingNotes.title: Passing Notes (Chromaticism)
passingNotes.desc: Scales contain the 'right' notes, but it's the passages between them that create interest. Adding chromatic passing notes (a half step above or below a target note) adds sophistication. Bebop Dominant is a scale that formalizes this concept by adding a chromatic passing note between b7 and the root.

guide.title: Scale Guide
guide.subtitle: Discover the origin, character and secrets of every scale

majorIonian.origin: The oldest and most universal scale in Western music, codifying the natural resonance of a vibrating string.
majorIonian.character: Bright, open and balanced; it conveys stability and pleasantness, like a sunrise.
majorIonian.theory: Made of whole tones except between degrees 3-4 and 7-8 (the two semitones that define its bright quality).
majorIonian.tips: The default choice for major-key songs; works over major triads (I, IV, V) and their seventh extensions.

naturalMinorAeolian.origin: One of the seven natural modes of the Major scale, appearing in European folk music since the Middle Ages.
naturalMinorAeolian.character: Melancholic and introspective; it evokes sadness without drama, like a quiet lament.
naturalMinorAeolian.theory: Like Major but with a lowered 3rd, 6th and 7th, creating the characteristic minor sound.
naturalMinorAeolian.tips: Essential for minor-key songs, ballads and emotional rock ballads; fit with i, iv, VII chords.

majorPentatonic.origin: A five-note simplification of the Major scale found in folk traditions worldwide, from Celtic to Asian music.
majorPentatonic.character: Fresh and direct, with no dissonances; it sounds open and accessible, like a mountain melody.
majorPentatonic.theory: Major scale stripped of the 4th and 7th degrees, removing the semitone clashes for a pure sound.
majorPentatonic.tips: Perfect for lead melodies in pop, country and rock; hard to make mistakes when improvising.

minorPentatonic.origin: The backbone of African-American blues, developed in the American South from the contact of African and European traditions.
minorPentatonic.character: Warm and expressive with an inherent tension; it feels soulful and instantly recognizable.
minorPentatonic.theory: Natural Minor without the 2nd and 6th degrees, leaving only the most stable notes of the minor mode.
minorPentatonic.tips: The go-to scale for blues and rock solos; pair it with the Major Pentatonic for the classic sweet-bitter contrast.

blues.origin: Born in the African-American communities of the Deep South in the late 19th century as a vehicle for emotional expression.
blues.character: Powerful and raw, with a bittersweet tension that creates an irresistible groove; it speaks directly to the heart.
blues.theory: Minor Pentatonic with an added b5 (blue note), creating a distinctive dissonance that defines the blues sound.
blues.tips: Ideal for expressive solos in blues, rock and funk; use the blue note as a passing tone for authentic phrasing.

mixolydian.origin: The fifth mode of the Major scale, used in medieval church music and rediscovered in rock and roll in the 1950s.
mixolydian.character: Major yet slightly melancholic; a 'happy minor' that feels festive but with an underlying restlessness.
mixolydian.theory: Like Major but with a lowered 7th (b7 instead of 7), removing the leading tone and adding a bluesy flavor.
mixolydian.tips: The essential scale for dominant 7th chords (V7) in jazz and blues; rock classics like Sweet Child O' Mine are built on it.

dorian.origin: The second mode of the Major scale, prominent in Greek antiquity and revived in jazz via bebop and hard bop.
dorian.character: Sophisticated and cool, it sounds like a minor scale with a bright twist; elegant without being flashy.
dorian.theory: Like Natural Minor but with a raised 6th degree, giving it a brighter, more complex color than Aeolian.
dorian.tips: Perfect for minor chords that need a touch of brightness; works beautifully over i7 and iv7 progressions.

phrygian.origin: The third mode of the Major scale, named after an ancient Greek region and central to flamenco guitar tradition.
phrygian.character: Dark and exotic, with a Spanish or Middle Eastern flavor; it conveys mystery and intensity.
phrygian.theory: Like Natural Minor but with a lowered 2nd degree, creating a characteristic minor-second interval near the root.
phrygian.tips: Great for flamenco, metal and neoclassical pieces; use it over i and bII chords for a dark, dramatic effect.

lydian.origin: The fourth mode of the Major scale, considered by the ancient Greeks to represent the 'most perfect harmony' of the cosmos.
lydian.character: Dreamy and floating, it sounds elevated and transcendent; like rising above the clouds.
lydian.theory: Like Major but with a raised 4th degree (#4), creating a luminous, aspirational quality absent in the standard major scale.
lydian.tips: Adds magic to major chords; use over Imaj7 and Imaj9 to create a cinematic, ethereal atmosphere.

locrian.origin: The seventh mode of the Major scale, rarely used as a tonal center due to its extreme instability since medieval times.
locrian.character: Tense and unstable, it feels suspended and unresolved; like standing on the edge of a cliff.
locrian.theory: The most dissonant natural mode, characterized by a b2 and b5 near the root, creating maximum tension.
locrian.tips: Best used as a passing color over diminished or half-diminished chords; avoid as a tonal center.

harmonicMinor.origin: Created in the 18th century to restore the leading tone in minor keys, bridging classical harmony and exotic Eastern sounds.
harmonicMinor.character: Mysterious and exotic, with an Arabesque quality; it evokes ancient courts and dramatic tension.
harmonicMinor.theory: Natural Minor with a raised 7th degree, creating a large leap (3 semitones) between the 6th and 7th degrees.
harmonicMinor.tips: Ideal for neoclassical pieces, metal and Eastern-inspired melodies; works over i, III, VI, VII chords.

melodicMinor.origin: Developed in the 18th century to smooth the melodic motion in minor keys, later adopted as the basis for jazz harmony.
melodicMinor.character: Elegant and refined, it blends minor gravity with a clear, soaring quality; like a noble knight's theme.
melodicMinor.theory: Natural Minor with raised 6th and 7th degrees (ascending form), making it sound like a major scale with a b3.
melodicMinor.tips: The parent scale of jazz improvisation; essential for over i7, ii7, and altered dominants in jazz and fusion.

lydianDominant.origin: A synthetic scale (not from the Major scale) popularized in jazz for its dreamy yet unstable quality over dominant chords.
lydianDominant.character: Bubbly and kaleidoscopic, it sounds like a major scale refracted through a crystal; playful yet unresolved.
lydianDominant.theory: Like Lydian (raised 4th) but with a b7 instead of the major 7th, combining brightness with dominant tension.
lydianDominant.tips: Play over dominant 7th chords that resolve to iim7; adds a modern, luminous sound to jazz and fusion.

altered.origin: Derived from Super Locrian by omitting the P5, it became central to jazz after the bebop era as the ultimate altered dominant scale.
altered.character: Ultra-tense and chromatic, it sounds like a storm of notes in constant motion; chaotic but controlled.
altered.theory: Like Locrian but with raised 1st and 2nd degrees, removing the perfect 5th and adding multiple tensions (b9, #9, #11, b13).
altered.tips: The go-to scale for dominant 7th chords needing altered tensions (b9, #9, #11, b13); use sparingly for dramatic effect.

wholeTone.origin: A symmetric six-note scale discovered in the 19th century, prized by Impressionist composers like Debussy for its dreamlike quality.
wholeTone.character: Ethereal and floating, it dissolves the sense of tonality creating an atmosphere of suspended wonder.
wholeTone.theory: All intervals are whole tones (2 semitones), making it symmetric and ambiguous; only two transpositions exist.
wholeTone.tips: Use for ambient, Impressionist or surrealist passages; works well over augmented chords (C+, C7+) as a colorful fill.

halfWholeDiminished.origin: A symmetric eight-note scale born in bebop jazz, used as an organized way to navigate the chromatic notes of a dominant chord.
halfWholeDiminished.character: Dense and restless, it sounds like a cascade of falling dominoes; intense but controlled.
halfWholeDiminished.theory: Starts with a semitone then whole tone alternating (H-W), creating maximum chromatic movement across all eight notes.
halfWholeDiminished.tips: Effective over altered dominants and as a dominant substitute; great for bebop lines and chromatic runs.

bebopDominant.origin: Created by bebop pioneers in the 1940s by adding a chromatic passing tone to the Mixolydian scale for more fluid improvisations.
bebopDominant.character: Energetic and propulsive, it swings with a relentless forward motion; the sound of a party in full swing.
bebopDominant.theory: Mixolydian scale with an added major 7th (before the root) acting as a chromatic passing note to smooth the b7-root resolution.
bebopDominant.tips: Designed for fast dominant-chord passages; adds drive and momentum to jazz and bebop improvisation.
